preFix.title: Green hazel bean-pole arch
preFix.subtitle: A tunnel arch for runner beans from hazel coppice poles
preFix.excerpt: A tunnel arch for runner beans made from paired hazel coppice poles lashed and woven together at the top. Two rows of poles arch over to meet in the centre; the beans climb both sides. Hazel bends when green without splitting.
preFix.sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for the wattle arch structure; Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) for the kitchen garden bean-arch form.

content.text: A hazel arch over a bed of runner beans grows the beans upward and over, creating a tunnel of pods to harvest from. Two rows of
green

hazel poles are pushed into the ground on either side of the bed, bent toward each other, and lashed at the apex. The lateral weave between the poles gives the structure rigidity and also provides more climbing surface. This is a beginner project: the only tools are a
billhook
for trimming side shoots and secateurs for the lashing withies.
Wood

Hazel coppice poles are the standard: straight, flexible when green, and available in lengths of 2 to 3 metres from coppice woodlands throughout Britain. The arch poles need to be 10 to 14 mm in diameter and 2.4 metres long. Thicker poles are harder to bend into the arch; thinner ones may not support the weight of a full bean crop in a wet summer. The lashing withies are thin flexible hazel rods 4 to 6 mm in diameter from the same coppice cut.
Tools
Billhook, Yorkshire pattern: for clearing side shoots from the hazel poles before use.
Secateurs: for cutting the thin lashing withies and trimming ends.
Bow saw (optional): for crosscutting poles to a consistent length.
Layout

Mark two rows of holes along the bed sides, spaced 300 mm apart in each row and 600 mm between the rows. A standard garden bed is 900 to 1200 mm wide; the row spacing can increase slightly to 750 mm for a wider bed. A bed 2.4 metres long takes eight pairs of poles giving eight arches.
Planting the poles

Sharpen the bottom of each pole to a point with the billhook. Push the point 300 mm into the soil beside the bed using body weight; no mallet is needed in prepared garden soil. Push all poles of one row first, then the opposite row.
Bending and lashing the arch

Take one pole from each row that faces each other. Bend the top of each pole toward the centre of the bed in a gentle curve and overlap the tops by 150 mm. Green hazel bends without cracking; if the pole resists, work the bend slowly, easing it over rather than forcing it. Lash the crossed tops with a thin withy: two or three tight turns around both poles, then a half-hitch to lock. Each pair of poles forms one arch.

Once all arches are formed, weave two or three horizontal hazel rods through the arches at 400 mm intervals up from the ground. Push the end of each horizontal rod behind the first arch, in front of the second, behind the third, and so on. The horizontal weave is done in an alternating over-and-under pattern and gives the whole structure its lateral stiffness.
Planting the beans

Sow or plant runner beans at the base of each pole, one bean per pole. The bean will find the pole within a few days of germination; guide the initial shoot around the pole if it goes astray. By mid-summer the arch will be fully clothed.
Variations
Wigwam: six poles pushed into a circle 600 mm diameter, bent in and lashed at a single apex, for a compact climbing structure for peas or sweet peas.
Permanent path arch: larger diameter poles (20 mm) and a wider span (1200 mm) for a walkway arch over a path.
Ash for heavier crops: ash poles at 14 to 16 mm are slightly stiffer than hazel but support heavy squash or climbing courgettes better.
Care

At the end of the season, clear the bean plants, pull the poles from the ground, and dry them under cover for next year. Poles that have dried and hardened in their arch shape can be re-used for two to three seasons if stored dry.
Troubleshooter

attrs.fix: Use poles cut within the previous two to three weeks. A pole dried for more than four weeks will crack on a sharp bend. If a knot is present in the top third of the pole, it will be the weak point; cut the pole to use the knot-free section as a horizontal weaver instead.
attrs.cause: Pole too dry or a knot in the bending zone
attrs.symptom: A pole snapped when bending into the arch

attrs.fix: Add horizontal weavers at 200 mm intervals up from the ground. Poles should be pushed 300 mm into the soil minimum; if the ground is hard, use a steel bar to pre-punch the holes.
attrs.cause: Too few horizontal weavers or poles not pushed deep enough
attrs.symptom: The structure sways in the wind
